"Nach Beobachtungen der Brüder Faaßen, Kaldenkirchen, zieht die vom Prinzendyk herkommende Römerstraße Tüddern - Xanten schnurgerade weiter in nordöstl. Richtung durch den Kaldenkirchener Wald, auf kurze Strecken als Kiesstreifen kenntlich, gelegentlich auch durch seitliche Materialgruben bezeichnet, bis zum Galgenvenn, wo sie 1961 geschnitten wurde. Hier macht sie einen schwachen Knick und führt nun in Richtung NNO wiederum schnurgerade durch den Wald und das Rodungsgelände, durch den Südostzipfel der Ziegelei Teeuwen. Weiterhin ist sie nicht mehr kenntlich, wird von der Landstraße nach Venlo und der Eisenbahn überschritten und tritt bei dem Hof Kleinenbrand in der Gemarkung Leuth wieder in Erscheinung (vgl. Bracht 53 und Leuth 15). Das im Fragebogen 1925 genannte alte Straßenpflaster im oberen Galgenvenn konnte Erscheinung (vgl. Bracht 53 und Leuth 15). Das im Fragebogen 1925 genannte alte Straßenpflaster im oberen Galgenvenn konnte nicht mehr ermittelt werden."

terp attested. Habitation ended in ROMM, location used as burial site between 400-750
terp attested. Apparently a source of one or more bronze figurines? But literature not found

terp attested. Coordinates moved to location of Midlum-Noord cf ARC 2011-71. other finds belong to periphery of habitation at Midlum-Noord cf RAAP 3299t. Skeleton burial in squatting position found approx 220 m to the east of this site location (BROB 1950-21 2)
not sure if settlement, report says that all finds indicate LME habitation but does not explain presence of 14 sherds dated IJZL-ROMM

terp attested. Actually two terps, 125 m between the centre points. Another nearby terp (vindplaats 10) is located too far to be part of this site (280 m) and has not enough Roman finds to be own site
terp attested. Coordinates changed based on report
terp attested. Part of row of five terps, along with site 7877
terp attested. Part of row of five terps, along with site 7877
terp attested. Roman dating unclear, only finds found in younger ditches, no Roman-dated features found yet
terp attested. Roman dating unclear, only finds found in younger ditches, no Roman-dated features found yet
terp attested. Part of row of five terps, along with site 7867
terp attested. Actually three terps in close proximity. Other terps in surroundings also have finds dated IJZL-ROM but not enough to qualify as site
terp attested. Northern dorpsterp of Stiens
terp attested. Coordinates changed to location of findspot 1087100
coordinates moved to location of find spot 1086596. this research found ditches from land use related to the neighbouring terp, which is situated at the aforementioned find spot
terp attested. Coordinates changed based on report
terp attested. Four terps very close to each other
includes all finds for Zuiderloo from the trial trenches (Dian 100), including those that belong to sites 7932 and 7941

coordinates changed to centre of vindplaats 1-5. original coordinates were centre coordinates for entire De Druppels research area, which includes vindplaats 6-11 (site ID 8043). Both sites are on separate high tidal levees, with a lower lying levee inbetween. Archis-location 1097713 contains finds from both vindplaats 1-5 and vindplaats 6-11, are not separated in the database. habitation dated 1st-3rd century. Some inhumation remains but in weird contexts? at least partly enclosed with ditch and palisade
five-sided enclosed terrain with (probable) watchtower and two other secondary structures, with lots of material indicating military presence contemporary with Velsen fort

find location 1077623 merged with site 7940, because coordinates in Archis are incorrect (this find location belongs to an excavation which succeeded earlier research at the location of site 7940). Site 7933 is maintained because find location 1058104, although with fewer (unknown number of) finds qualifies as a site on its own as well. coordinates of site 7933 moved to this find location (originally site 7933 was situated at coordinates of find location 1077623)
occupation dated between AD 39 and 47 based on coin finds (MLR 38)
dated -in and around the 3rd century- (cf Steek 2012-03-02U). Includes three contemporary inhumation burials
habitation difficult to date, most features affected by post-Roman erosion
habitation dated from IJZL until 3rd century
dated 200 BC-AD 100, settlement estimated 750 m by 80-150 m in size
IJZL habitation found in excavation, no traces of Roman habitation and some older sherds that were dated to ROM may actually be IJZL as well (cf RAAP 3402)
sherds dated mostly 1st century. Features associated with agriculture and animal husbandry, but amount of finds suggest habitation nearby
most finds indicate habitation in IJZM-IJZL, with probable second phase of habitation in 2nd century
finds indicate most likely ROMV-ROMM dating, with exception of ROML-MEVA fibula. Report (AAC 41) only gives Roman as dating
habitation probably until AD 100, possibly extending somewhat into 2nd century

habitation dated 2nd-3rd century, possibly continuing into 4th century. One inhumation, shallow grave, human lying face down, possibly 3rd century but not stated clearly in the text (AKH 1996 350-352). Includes coin hoard dated AD 222-268 (also called Schagen-Witte Paal VI in Archis)
possibly one long line of habitation features from site 8052-8007-8326

site described as dating to IJZL-ROM, but -no indication of 4th century habitation- suggests that habitation ended already in ROMM. Two inhumation burials dated to ROMM-ROML, although the younger one was not burried properly (execution?), among older (IJZL) and younger burials (ME). a number of remarkable metal finds suggest site has been used as a cult site (offerplaats) in 4th-7th century (ROML-VME)

wells dated 2nd-3rd century found on find locations 1071606 and 1071628 near Lindengracht, find location 1071921 on Canadaplein and find location 1041961 near Hooge Huijs. Find location 1111875 (Grote of St. Laurenskerk) actually contains mostly spolia (AHR 29 33-38), except for one in situ pit with Roman sherds. this find location is kept as centre coordinates for this site on the basis of being central between the various well finds
habitation dated 50 BC-3rd century AD, with preceding habitation in IJZM (550-250 BC)
dated around AD 300. Four inhumations including one infant and seven cremations, as well as animal inhumations. Also hearths with ironslags indicating ironworking
coordinates changed to location of terp excavation, where habitation is attested in 2nd-3rd century. Finds from original coordinates (find location 1040811) are interpreted as not being in situ
one inhumation of a woman in shallow grave (hurkgraf). Indications for iron production including oven

coordinates changed to centre of vindplaats 6-11 of De Druppels research area, which includes vindplaats 1-5 (site ID 7930). original coordinates were coordinates from preliminary coring research (find location 1094334). Both sites are on separate high tidal levees, with a lower lying levee inbetween. Archis-location 1097713 (associated with site 7930) contains finds from both vindplaats 1-5 and vindplaats 6-11, are not separated in the database. habitation dated 1st-3rd century
start of occupation AD 15-16, occupation ended around AD 45-47. coordinates moved to centre location of first two phases (was originally placed to the east, just outside the fort)
no clear houseplans found, habitation occurred on peat which has mostly disappeared now, only features of deepest pits remain

first use of terrain in the 2nd century or late 1st century, with possible sedentary habitation in 2nd half of 2nd century (ROMMB). Habitation attested (through houseplans) for AD 260-330 (ROMMB-ROMLA), with palisade enclosure and contemporary burial site (min. 6 inhumation burials). one additional inhumation dated to second half of 4th century (ROMLB), and also some other undated burials, cremations and human bone remains spread throughout the settlement that may have been left out in the open (signs of wear from movement and dog chewing)
habitation features dated to IJZL-ROMV, although coin finds also indicate 2nd-3rd century presence
only 1st century finds, absence of 2nd-3rd century noted by original researchers. Finds found in younger castle moat but assumed to belong to Roman settlement in the vicinity of the castle
IJZL house plan found, and indications for habitation lasting into 3rd-4th century, although actual ROM house plans are probably mostly still unexcavated
possibly just ROMV-ROMM based on interpretation of ditches
possibly just IJZL (AMK-monument does not mention ROM finds)
habitation features attested, finds not dated precisely
coordinates of RAAP 9 catalogusnummer 55 do not match exactly with this find location, but this find location does match the presented location on the map in the appendix of RAAP 9
coordinates changed to location of ROB excavation (find location 1044644)
coordinates changed to location of site as described in ADC 179. find location 1071949 (just one Roman sherd) removed due to being too far from this new location and also probably not in situ
find locations 1047213, 1047215, 1047216, 1047220 moved to site 8297 due to being part of that AMK-monument
in Early Medieval Period this becomes part of larger settlement of Limmen-De Krocht (together with site 7976)
indications for habitation from early La Tene to 3rd century, finds very poorly registered in Archis
pottery suggests dating in 2nd century. Coordinates may not be accurate, perhaps same site as finds from site 8120?
possibly just ROMV, some find locations only have ROMV finds but the most finds are dated just ROM
excavation Gerrit van Assendelftstraat only found plough marks, but separate find spot Starstraat with Roman pottery indicates that habitation is likely nearby
terra sigillata dated 2nd century and 1st half of 3rd century, other sherds dated probably 2nd century
pottery dated 1st century, interpreted as burial site according to Archis? Check with literature reference (AJ 3 129-132)
habitation in period AD 1-200, which the text refers to as the Early Roman Period, but should probably encompass ROMM as well
trial trench yielded indeterminate pits and a ditch system, not certain if they belong to habitation
rope around pottery AMS-dated to AD 21-170, pottery appears to be 2nd half of 1st century
coring research revealed deep disturbance of the soil profile, concluding that no features will be present anymore. although presence and amount of sherds may suggest habitation here or nearby
coordinates changed to near Strandpaal 69, according to description
sherds found in arable layer, possible indication of habitation nearby
coordinates changed to those of find location 1059224, as finds from find location 1057467 might not be in situ
sherds not registered in Archis, only features
report of excavation only concludes IJZM habitation, but presence of IJZL-ROM sherds indicate ROM habitation as well, with the corresponding features probably lost to modern disturbance
finds dated IJZ-ROM, but text describes features as -most likely dating to ROM, possibly slightly before-, implying only the end of the Iron Age
possible habitation or other activities at this location dated AD 270-725, peat extraction certain
two features (pits) but not sure if it belongs to habitation, one pit also contains one single cremation bone fragment (alongside pottery sherds). Features of peat extraction certainly pressent
coordinates changed to find location 1061025, closer to designated AMK-monument where the supposed centre of this site is according to RAAP 73
number of military finds, but no excavation so context is missing
literature related to find spot 1042860 does not mention finds, but finds are registered for find spot 1106235 (without literature)
interpreted as burial site according to Archis? Check with literature reference (AJ 3 129-132)
ditches from parcellation, possibly belonging to a single complex with sites 8317 and 8154?
no Roman finds mentioned in literature, but Archis find location mentions Roman habitation layer dated to ROMLA
some pits with Roman sherds, not paid much attention due to presence of important Neolithic site and presence of Medieval features (some Roman features may have been mistaken as Medieval ones)

ritual deposition of material on a sandy ridge near peat, starting in Late Iron Age (or IJZM) and continuing in Roman Period until at least 3rd century, possibly 4th century. Finds very poorly registered in Archis
preceding habitation in IJZV-IJZM, but Roman habitation started in ROMV, larger settlement in ROMM. cremation burials in ROMM and ROML. Horreum in ROMM
two phases: IJZL-ROMV and  ROMMB-VMEB. Burial consists of one cremation grave dated to ROML, found 300 m south of contemporary settlement, near location of IJZL-ROMV habitation

Struikakkers - ROML habitation part of larger settlement, also evidence for IJZL-ROMV habitation (ADC 4901/5627). Dirk Staalweg - ROML habitation (BROB 12 25-38). Coordinates moved from 1373601 to 1263958. find location 1098586 moved from site 9264
find location 1373556 only contains IJZ and LME habitation (cf LAR 1104)
situated on riverdune. Characteristics of elite settlement in ROML
based on find material possibly two phases: IJZL-ROMV and ROML
two sites within 270 m (site ID 9226 and 8843), merged because of proximity and similar date. Both were investigated by ROB (A. de Haan) but report not findable
confusingly (see 8718), also known as Didam-Aalsbergen, Didam-Kollenburg and Didam-Kollenburg Noord
sherds related to habitation dated IJZ-ROM, could potentially only be IJZL. Cremation burials dated to ROMM, located approx 180 m south of this site location
no habitation features found, but quantity of material suggests habitation in vicinity
houseplan is dated IJZL by researchers but find material in those features is dated IJZL-ROM
five cremation graves, one circular ditch potentially belonging to (disappeared) burial mound. Within IJZL settlement

water well (initially interpreted as waterhole) dug into the peat, terminus post quem ROMLB-VMEA. Unclear if part of rural settlement, more likely off-site structure for animal husbandry in vicinity of settlement
settlement merged with 8466 as they are interpreted as part of the same settlement. cemetery kept separate here (also has no Archis-records)
periphery of settlement, core settlement probably located a bit to the west of this site location
off-site structures, but should be near ROMV habitation. Habitation moved to higher parts after ROMV due to increasing wetness, for example to site 9474
interpretation very uncertain, finds only described as 'urnen'
habitation uncertain, but pottery finds indicate activity around (potentially older) burial mound
on riverdune, found during sand extraction
9 cremation graves with one being inside terra sigillata urn dated to AD 200

finds include bronze plate with inscription and assorted pottery, and features include a well and assorted (post)holes, indicating habitation. Dated to Middle Roman Period-Late Medieval Period A (Volle Middeleeuwen), so dating precision is given as 1 even though Roman Period is specified to second level
AMK-monument is dated to IJZ, but potentially broader. Finds include sherds dated to ROM, and a bronze Mercurius statuette dating to ROMV-ROMM
one terra sigillata urn with ash, supposedly used for a burial

settlement possibly starting from AD 200, two cremation burials dated late 3rd-5th century, watchtower dated around AD 300. new publication by Habermehl forthcoming (as of February 2024). Finds not yet registered adequately
IJZ-ROM settlement supposed on the basis of 20 sherds found during coring research, no features excavated
dating based on pottery finds, no features reported, no literature available
location uncertain (based on old letters), and exact number of finds unknown
former AMK-monument, but has been delisted most likely due to destruction of the site. Unknown what the original site encompassed

dated from 80 to 225. one Roman-type horreum and other storage facilities. Settlement started with local building traditions, initially three contemporary homesteads, quickly growing to four contemporary homestead, and replaced by Roman building traditions around middle of 2nd century. Large part of settlement area remains unexcavated though. One stone-built structure that could be a villa, mansio or temple. wooden waterworks (quays and revetment) along 16 m-wide creek. whole assemblage suggests site to be an emporium or local trade centre along a natural (but reinforced) creek, that may have even formed part of an inland Scheldt-Meuse connection

possible military site ((e.g. castellum or fortlet) due to mention of straight roads and foundations of large houses) or some kind of large settlement, possibly related to site 10270. coordinates not precise (exact location unknown, site was outside of the coast in medieval and early modern period)
may be related to nearby site 10512

excavations at Ellewoutsdijk-Steendammeweg revealed nine houseplans from middle of 1st century to middle of 2nd century, two to four contemporary homesteads. Habitation area stretches out over approximately 500 m, in two clusters 350 m apart. Site is therefore split in two, with seven houseplans belonging to site 10899. coordinates moved to centre of this part of the site (vindplaats 4 in ADC 200)
salt extraction site with also habitation refuse indicating habitation nearby, dating to first quarter of 3rd century

excavation at Smokkelhoek yielded one houseplan, begin date in first half of 3rd century, ending between 275-300. there are also indications for habitation in late 2nd century approximately 80 m to the northwest at Biezelinge site, but any features are largely lost due to post-Roman erosion

wooden fortification measuring 150x240 m built around AD 175 after Chauci invasions in AD 172-174, situated on east bank of creek. second phase rebuilt in AD 190 with earthen wall. second phase destructed around AD 240 and a third phase with stone wall, round towers and moat was built around AD 260, lasting until around AD 290 after which the site was abandoned. also stone temple dating to first quarter of 3rd century. civil habitation around the fort with roads (to S, E and W), cremation burials and lime industry (burning of shells). coordinates moved to centre of site (based on supposed main building of fort)
coordinates moved, vindplaats 4 from ADC 1384 is much further south than the registered coordinates with Archis-find location 1101842. habitation dated in first half of 3rd century
site dated to late 1st century, with indications for salt extraction
coordinates moved to centre of vicus according to HAS 3. finds from excavations at Peurssensstraat (Bodac A0010) are periphery of this area
possible reoccupation in ROMLA, but not certain
pottery dates site in 2nd century, with possible indications for salt extraction or lime-burning. Settlement largely destroyed in short period between coring research and excavation
coordinates moved to centre of vicus according to HAS 3. vicus with industrial activity (lime-burning?)
remains of Roman settlement destroyed by medieval peat extraction, dated ROMM probably 2nd century
one houseplan dated first half of 1st century
habitation between AD 200-250, possibly seasonal (for gathering mussels)
habitation (potentially seasonally) from AD 150 until somewhere in 3rd century
harbour area, possibly also industrial area
remains of Roman settlement destroyed by medieval peat extraction

sherds dated to 2nd half of 2nd century and or 1st half of 3rd century. According to description of AMK-monument 2412 these finds belong to site 10940, but they are actually a separate site if newspaper article (EB 7-1-1988 5) is interpreted correctly
lime-burning site dated to fourth quarter of 1st century. No indication for habitation
find location 1090256 contains no Roman finds (ADC 1219), therefore coordinates were moved to find location 1087616
possibly military site (or military site nearby) based on rooftiles with stamps of classis germanica, similar to site 10948
pottery dates to 2nd-3rd century. Peat extraction with habitation nearby?
find location 1030833 moved to site 10925 as associated literature suggests it should be from that location. Ovens from lime-burning dated to 3rd quarter of 2nd century
habitation started end of 1st century and ended with fire in 2nd half of 2nd century
also peat extraction pits and indications for lime-burning due to presence of lime blocks
possibly military site (or military site nearby) based on rooftiles with stamps of classis germanica, similar to site 10931
Roman finds in secondary context, but possible that a Roman settlement was disturbed by medieval peat extraction
pottery dates to 2nd-3rd century. Potential habitation near peat extraction pits
no features of habitation but presence of pottery suggests habitation in the direct vicinity
settlement where pottery dates between AD 100-200, largely destroyed by peat extraction
potential location of lime-burning, no indication of habitation as far as can be deduced from Archis
no features of habitation found but presence of find material indicate habitation nearby
coordinates moved to find location 1111149, which is the attested site of cremation remains. Date set as contemporary to castellum, needs to be checked with HAS 3
find location 1026342 moved to site 10913 as associated literature references belong to that site
possible small military fortification (praesidium?) according to HAS 3. date set as contemporary to castellum, needs to be checked with HAS 3
Roman sherds found ex-situ in late medieval peat extraction pit, but amount of sherds suggests Roman habitation nearby
possibly ex-situ? (sherds found in younger mound)

finds from survey in 1947. Archis-description states that no features were found after check by ROB in 1998, but not clear if features were entirely absent or could have been present (but lost due to peat extraction)

habitation starts around AD 10. roughly 4 contemporary households in AD 10-180 interval, roughly five in 180-270 interval. First phase of villa is constructed in early 2nd century and villa is abandoned around middle of 3rd century. Hiddink (ZAR 53) believes the settlement was already a proto-villa from its onset due to the enclosure, orientation of buildings and location of main building, indicative of a precise structuring of the settlement, akin to Oss-Westerveld (also called enclosed rural settlement by Slofstra). finally, a nearby structure was interpreted as a cult site (sanctuary) contemporary to the settlement, but interpretation is not certain according to Hiddink

3 houseplans in IJZM-IJZL (with one possibly IJZL-ROMV), 20 ROMV-ROMM houseplans. continuous habitation supposed from late IJZL until ROMMB. road and enclosure ditch present from around AD 100 (road stopped being in use around AD 200), change to axial settlement around AD 200 with rooftiles in one structure, with proto-villa supposed to the west of the excavated area, with an economy based on sheep (wool production). habitation lasts until beginning of 3rd century (AD 225?), no indication of habitation in late 3rd century. one possible burial monument dated around end of 2nd century or beginning of 3rd century, with potential larger cemetery extending westwards outside excavated area

three areas of habitation within 300 m of each other, can probably be considered a single settlement. Coordinates changed to one of the habitation areas (vindplaats 6 of ZAN 266). Original coordinates were central coordinates for the entire research area and did not reflect Roman find locations. continuous habitation from IJZL until 3rd century, with a quick abandonment somewhere in the 3rd century (probably before AD 280)

preceding Iron Age habitation ended in IJZL, habitation restarted in 2nd century in slightly different location (separated from Iron Age location). At least eleven houseplans, possibly two contemporary although not certain as part of settlement may be outside excavated area. some buildings will have had rooftiles and possibly window glass and natural stone building material. habitation ended at the end of 3rd century

29 houseplans dating from 1st half of 1st century until 2nd half of 3rd century. Report (Archol 280) speaks of three habitation areas, although they are all within 250 m and could be considered a single settlement. Phase 1 (AD 0-100) and 2 (end 1st-end 2nd century) have 3 contemporary houses, phase 3 (end 2nd-3rd century) has 6 contemporary houses (2-3 in two habitation areas each, 1 in another habitation area). on the basis of this distinction, in this database the site is considered a regular rural settlement from ROMVA-ROMMA, and a large rural settlement from ROMMB-ROMLA (until end of 3rd century). unclear how many houseplans remain in unexcavated area. burial site with ditch structures and cremation burials, of which one-third dates to Roman Period (mostly ROMV) with others dating to IJZM and IJZL.

habitation from IJZL until middle of 2nd century, with seventeen houseplans dating to ROM, as well as a horreum. researchers found three 50-year phases (0-50, 50-100, 100-150) with 5-6 houseplans per phase. also 6 contemporary houseplans in preceding IJZL. burial site dated to IJZM-BRONSL directly east and southeast of habitation, with five Roman burials located much further to the east (site 11179) but continuous with the burial site extending all the way to the habitation area of this site
28 houseplans dating to ROM, next to earlier IJZ settlement. Two horrea. Habitation started around 85 BC and was probably continuous into ROMV, and lasted until around AD 260

first phase consists of two zwervende erven in IJZL-ROMV (ZAN 183). rural settlement in excavated area of ZAR 83, 150-200 m to the northeast (location of this site), started in middle of 1st century, continuous habitation with 2-3 homesteads with 1-2 houses per homestead. End of habitation around end of 3rd century. Settlement also extends outside of excavated area of ZAR 83, with three houseplans in excavated area of ZAN 913/Steek 2016-01-05

74 ditch structures and pits with cremations. Dating mostly to 1st and 2nd century, ending in early 3rd century. Three nearby structures that might be interpreted as spiekers but could also be structures related to the burial site
fifteen houseplans dating between AD 10-250 (three contemporary houseyards). Also charcoal pits for production of charcoal for ironworking
coin hoard from 3rd-4th century, but also indication for stone-built rural settlement (tuff, rooftiles, etc.)
three houseplans dated from beginning of 1st century until middle of 2nd century in ITHO 8.2 report
eightteen houseplans, probably one contemporary house in 1st century, 2.6 contemporary houses in 2nd-3rd century. Habitation lasted between roughtly AD 30 to AD 260
twelve houseplans dated AD 150-270 on the basis of pottery

38 houseplans, 4-5 contemporary. continuous habitation in three phases from AD 1-10 to AD 260. eight burial ditch structures and a number of other burial-related features (remains of burials and secondary interment). One structure dated after AD 15, maybe in second quarter of 1st century. other structures and features dated after AD 125

vindplaats 6 (225 cremations dating from IJZL to 225-250 AD, constructed around IJZM cemetery), vindplaats 7 (one isolated cremation dating to late 2nd or early 3rd century), vindplaats 8 (two cremations dated to end of ROMM or begin of ROML), vindplaats 9 (8 ROML cremations next to IJZV-IJZM cemetery), vindplaats 10 (one ROML cremation added to IJZ burial mound). finally, also one houseplan tentatively dated to ROM but no finds (vindplaats 24), located directly southwest of large IJZL-ROM cemetery
habitation features found in excavation dated to 2nd century, although finds from nearby find locations might indicate possible ROMV presence as well?
no clearly Roman features attested (most dated features belong to IJZM-IJZL habitation), but amount of Roman pottery suggests some of the undated features may be Roman
four houseplans. habitation started in second half of 4th century, was abandoned (shortly) after AD 450

supposed cult site with rooftiles in low-lying area (depression), dated to 2nd century on basis of ceramics. on the same site, a hutkom dated to the 3rd century was found, possibly also with rooftiles. four cremation burials located 140 m to the north of supposed cult site (site also called Son-Markt), two dated in 1st half of 2nd century, one in 2nd or 3rd century, one in middle of 3rd century

115 burial structures and burials, dated 50 BC-AD 225. part of burial site was disturbed, original number of burials estimated to be 185. one nearby house (huis 5 from De Groot 2001) dated around AD 200 (pottery dated 2nd half of 2nd century, well dated AD 203-210)

two houseplans dated from AD 150 to 3rd century. Rooftiles were found, which probably were used for a roof above the well. Burial site was used from 2nd half of 2nd century or 1st half of 3rd century until AD 250-300, around 18-20 burials were found in excavation. there is a preceding IJZV burial in the same cemetery and also IJZM-IJZL burials nearby

wet depression with deposition of mini-amphorae and coins, with possible small stone structure (temple?) near the Gertrudiskerk. Imported pottery dates cult site in 2nd of half 2nd century until 1st half of 3rd century. Features of habitation only found around the church, no date given but here set as contemporary to cult site
two houseplans, first one built in second half of 2nd century, second one built in first half of 3rd century

coordinates changed to location of Roman habitation, original coordinates were central coordinates for the entire research area. Eastern half of this site has three houseplans with two dating to ROMV and one to ROMMA. Western half has five houseplans dating to ROMMA-ROMMB

vindplaats 18 (centre of this site) has indications for ROMV habitation (water pit) but no houseplans dating to ROMV (supposedly outside excavated area), and three (possibly contemporary) houseplans dating to 2nd century. vindplaats 16 has three houseplans dating to IJZL-ROMV, located approx 230 m northeast of the centre of this site (vindplaat 18), with a potential fourth IJZ-ROM houseplan found in neighbouring trial trench research (Tran 4505)
four houseplans (huis 1-4 from De Groot 2001, huis 5 included in site 11052), habitation from AD 180 until 1st half of 3rd century
27 burial ditch structures and 3 cremation graves, dated between AD 100-200

earlier habitation occurred in IJZL, and ditch enclosure originates from that period as well. Later habitation occurred within existing ditch enclosure, starting around middle of 1st century, with more intense habitation from the middle of the 2nd century. habitation from this phase also cuts across enclosure, indicating it was at least partly closed. indications for secondary ironworking. habitation ended between AD 236 and 250

7 burial ditch structures and 2 cremation burials with rich inventories, although original burial site must have been larger (based on 19th-century finds nearby). Earliest burial ditch dated to early 1st century or just prior (ROMVA), cremation burials are the youngest, dating in the 3rd century. burial site was abandoned by middle of 3rd century, contemporary with nearby villa site (site 11018). indications of presence of tumulus with burial monument dated to Flavian or Trajan (around AD 100). this site also encompasses the nearby find location of Roman road or route, with a dam across the Kleine Beerze brook (find location 1088754, RAAP 2537). most wood from dam construction dates to ROMVB (around AD 54-64), although some wood is older and ditches are as well, indicating predecessor of brook crossing in IJZ and possibly Bronze Age (based on bronze axe depositions). end date of dam not known, here set to contemporary with burial site and nearby villa
thirteen houseplans dated ROMV-ROMM, mostly 2-3 contemporary houses. End of habitation probably between AD 260-270. eleven structures interpreted as horrea dated ROMV-ROMM
three houseplans, possible two contemporary, dating from roughly AD 1-100

includes excavations of Wegekker and Kruisstraat, for a total of 17-18 houseplans. Including unexcavated area, the total number of houseplans is estimated at 35, resulting in around 4 contemporary houses throughout the habitation period. Habitation starts in IJZL (one houseplan dated after 200 BC but before AD 70, and older than other houseplans that also date before AD 70) and lasts until AD 260-276 (in Kruisstraat) and AD 150-250 (in De Mierden)
possible also IJZL-ROMV, but no habitation features attested
two houseplans dated ROMM or 2nd-3rd century. Also habitation in IJZL, but only one sherd that may indicate possible habitation in ROMV
thirteen burial ditch structures, but most cremations were lost due to post-Roman disturbance. Dated AD 150-250

burials consist of 4 cremations and 4 party related edge structures, dated to IJZL-ROMV (mostly IJZL, but sherds in ditches indicate (ritual) activity in ROMV as well). Located directly east of habitation. Younger burials are expected further to the east, outside the excavated area. excavated habitation consists of one houseplan dated to IJZL and four other houseplans dating to 2nd century (possibly continuing into 3rd). more or less sedentary habitation is assumed from IJZL until 3rd century
no clear houseplans but various habitation phases, including in IJZ, ROMV and 3rd century. (dis)continuity unclear
supposed Gallo-Roman temple dated by Bogaers (NO 1) to 1st-3rd century on the basis of finds. Burial(s) undated but here assumed to be at most contemporary, but not younger

finds within AMK-monument indicate habitation somewhere in IJZ-ROM. Find location 1093938 moved to site 11049 (exactly 20 km to the east) as they belong to that site (x-coordinate wrong). Coordinates for this site moved to find location 1103419

(imported) pottery and building material found in medieval features, but seem to indicate presence of stone-built settlement nearby as other known stone-built settlement is too far. Dated 2nd half of 2nd century
finds cannot be dated more precisely than ROM, although ADC 139 states that early ROM is most likely
finds indicate presence of significant settlement. site dated between Flavian period and first half of 3rd century. One cremation burial
three houseplans with potstal, two dated between 160-170 and 190 (one of which has a portico), one dated only roughly to 2nd century
one waterpit dated to AD 420-575, indicative of habitation nearby in unexcavated area

also contains site of Someren-SRV Terrein. Eleven houseplans in Someren-SRV Terrein are tentatively dated to late 2nd and 3rd century, but also one well dated to AD 3-4 (ROMVA) indicating earlier habitation in the vicinity (but probably a single homestead rather than a concentration of habitation such as in the later phase). also roof tiles were found - stone-built rural settlement?
ten houseplans dated between 150 BC-AD 100. probably two contemporary houses
also supposed presence of large-scale pottery production (for regional market)

early interpretation of villa, although later research by Van der Weele states that cult site (with stone-built temple?) more likely. ditch enclosure attested. Activity starts in second half of 1st century and lasts until third quarter of 3rd century. Also some burial ditches nearby, for which dating is unclear, for now they are set as contemporary to the cult site. coordinates changed to find location 1059408 as this is closer to the centre of the terrain
not more than one contemporary houseplan, according to description of trial trenches in AMK-monument listing

find locations 1081565 and 1081566 moved to site 11187 as they belong to that site. Roman habitation unclear, although lots of Roman finds were recovered at late medieval site Kemenade and in Blixembosch area (Iron Age and Medieval site)
two houseplans, dated somewhere between 200 BC and first century AD and said to date around the transition from Iron Age to Roman Period
indications for habitation in direct vicinity dating to IJZ-ROM(V)
possibly next to a probably pre-Roman burial mound, but mound is not present anymore

five cremation burials dated to ROMV-ROMM on the edge of a BRONSL-IJZM burial site. The BRONSL-IJZM burial site extends all the way to the habitation area of site 11029, but the few Roman burials are quite distant from the habitation so constitute their own site. Burial site may extend further outside the excavated area, so more Roman burials might be present in the vicinity
cremation remains found, but pottery is more indicative of habitation, so assumed to be both burial site and settlement (with settlement located roughly 200 m to the south of the burial site)

find locations 1347745, 1025355, 1028604, 1107550 merged with site 11111 due to belonging to that site. Site 11186 then moved to coordinates of find location 1054124. RAAP 13 catalogusnummer 4 suggests this as a potential villa location and this is reiterated in the AMK-monument 2163, but only one rooftile was found, other building material was found at site 11111
one houseplan, pottery dated primarily 2nd century, maybe also late 1st and early 3rd century
nine houseplans, no more than three contemporary. habitation started around AD 375 and lasted until about AD 550

one houseplan, dated somewhere between AD 50-150. near IJZM-IJZL burial site
four or five houseplans, dating from 1st half of 1st century until 1st half of 2nd century (but mostly confined to 1st century, only one houseplan possibly extending into 2nd century)
three or four houseplans dated IJZL-ROMV
at least two houseplans dated IJZ-ROM (probably IJZL-ROM)
burial mound in celtic field? (tumulus in -wallenstructuur-)
dated 2nd half of 2nd century, but most features disappeared due to post-Roman disturbance, only one pit remained
interpretation as cult site based on AAS 22 229-243. coordinates changed to find location 1034701 as this more accurately represents the actual site location according to AAS 22 229-243
three houseplans but difficult to date due to lack of material
two houseplans, one houseplan dated probably 1st century and other houseplan dated 2nd century. Presence of rooftiles indicate stone-built
site dated AD 80-250 based on pottery
multiple features (including well, 14C-dated to AD 169-350) and indications of presence of Roman habitation, but no clear dating to settlement given. Site location also sometimes called HIVA-terrein

coordinates changed to location of eastern site of Lieshout-Beekseweg. Other two habitation sites were given new site IDs, but have no Archis-finds associated with them (the Archis-find location does not distinguish the three sites, but they are distant enough to be considered separate settlements). habitation started not long before AD 1, lasted until 3rd quarter of 2nd century. 21 houseplans, roughly 3 contemporary houses throughout the habitation duration. Two horrea
no habitation features found, but finds in multiple trenches and excavations indicate presence of habitation nearby
also called Houtse Beemd, where a late 1st or early 2nd century sealstone was found

water-based wooden construction, possibly reinforcement of bridgehead (wood not strong enough to support bridge itself) or some kind of dock or loading platform for small boats on the Dommel. 14C-dated to AD 75-240 and AD 355-545, indicating a longer duration of maintenance of this structure
two houseplans, first one built after AD 29, second after AD 130 with pottery datd around middle of 2nd century
pottery dates site in 1st century, possibly even before AD 50 entirely
find location 1040208 merged with site 11056, coordinates then changed to find location 1035833
one houseplan of rural settlement dated to 2nd century, namesake of the Alphen-Ekeren houseplan type. With rooftiles in excavation
one houseplan roughly dated to somewhere in first centuries AD based on pottery

originally interpreted as watchtower, now considered to be habitation with burial features (square enclosure of original watchtower with v-shaped ditch is also a supposed burial ditch?). Dated 1st-2nd century?
no features of habitation (settlement dated to IJZ), but ditches that are indicative of a Roman route
one houseplan and a hutkom, habitation possibly extending into ROMLA based on finds?

only one posthole with Roman sherd, building may also date IJZV instead of ROM
possibly related to site 11044, particularly the burial site
no Roman habitation features found (only IJZ habitation), but amount of Roman sherds indicate presence of habitation in vicinity
according to Archis description this is possibly a burial site on the basis of presence of a complete plate?
no clear houseplans but two outbuildings (spiekers) dated to IJZ-ROM. Coordinates changed to find location 1214948 where these structures were found in excavation

three houseplans, one undated, one dated to ROMV and one to late 1st or 2nd century (according to ZAR 42, 20). Also one inhumation burial in wooden coffin of ROMV dating in eastern edge of IJZ urnfield

one houseplan dating to ROMM, possibly ROMMA on the basis of absence of potstal
possibly just ROML based on finds

posthole with Roman sherds reported in Binck 1945. trial trenches 100 m to the south yielded no traces of Roman habitation (ADC 399 vindplaats 5) but this does not exclude Roman habitation at this site
finds dating mostly to 1st century according to original researchers
features of probably outbuilding indicate presence of habitation nearby
one probable houseplan and some other structures, but not dated more accurately than IJZ-ROM
coordinates moved to find location 1152531, which is in the centre of the three find locations

fourteen houseplans (and settlement possibly extending outside research area). Three houseplans dating to IJZL (250-12 BC), other houseplans dating to ROMV-ROML with habitation lasting until AD 400, but text is confusing about dating of houseplans (contradictions and non-correspondence with dates in provided table). No houseplans said to date to 2nd century. Burial site located roughly 200 m due east just across a small depression (ven), with two burial ditch structures dating to ROMV and two cremation burials dating to early 2nd century, located within larger BRONSL-IJZ cemetery. finds not registered in Archis
one houseplan (huis 38 in RAM 102) dating to ROMV and maybe older (end of IJZL). Coordinates moved to location of this house based on RAM 102 kaartbijlage 2. finds not registered in Archis
eight cremation burials dated 1st and 2nd century
coordinates changed to find location 1032648, where the literature (NKNOB 1970-4 44a) supposes the presence of habitation
no features of habitation but number of sherds (10+) indicate habitation nearby, probably higher on the coversand ridge
wood from bridge only 14C-dated, no dendro possible

dated 2nd half of 2nd century, but not clear what the interpretation of this site is. Could also be burial

at least one houseplan probably dated to ROM
one houseplan dated to late 2nd-3rd century

habitation features found in earlier excavation (Laarhoven 2001) not registered in Archis

remains of an oven. Possibly belongs to nearby habitation of site 11313

rural settlement with finds dating to 2nd century and 1st half of 3rd century according to APL 17 107-118 115, including rooftiles
called an urnfield in Archis, so possibly not Roman?

according to Archis description a large amount of sherds were found along a long zone from site 11185 until site 11083. this find location is in the middle of that elongated zone, but unsure if it is one large settlement or if there are multiple settlements
no clear features of habitation, but finds and a few features including charcoal pit indicate Roman activity nearby
possibly just IJZ, but RAAP 47 states limited Roman habitation was possible

coordinates moved to centre of habitation (vindplaats 4 in ZAR 55). Habitation consists of roughly 4-5 contemporary houseplans throughout the existence of the site, dated from at least AD 37 (possibly a little earlier) until AD 270. Site includes two cremation cemeteries to southeast of settlement, separated by small depression. Northern cemetery (vindplaats 2) contains 136 funerary structures (most are cremations but not all), dated from 1st half of 1st century until after AD 200 (but probably ended before AD 270). Southern cemetery (vindplaats 1) contains 13 cremations, and dates after AD 125 (but probably late 2nd century) and lasts until AD 270 at most

habitation dated from AD 75 until end of 3rd century. No preceding IJZL-ROMV habitation present. enclosed with ditch and palisade. Relatively rich villa, includes bathhouse, triclinium (heated dining hall), large amount of fragments of wall paintings

habitation dated AD 70-225, although some indication for earlier presence in ROMV (but no main buildings excavated). 22 main buildings, 4 secondary buildings and 5 spiekers. 2-6 contemporary houses. (Partially) enclosed between 70 and 125-150. one cremation burial (3rd quarter of 3rd century or younger) and one burial structure. Road departing from settlement traced for 220 m towards northeast

villa habitation started in 2nd half of 2nd century. Building with hypocaustum was possibly destroyed in 2nd half of 3rd century, although there are indications that terrain was used until 5th century (although exact type of use unclear). 6th-7th century burial site dug into floor of villa

168 cremation burials divided over two burial zones that are not that far apart (ca 100 m). 116 burials in zone 5 dating from 250 BC to AD 25-50, and 52 burials in zone 6 dating from 200 BC to AD 225. find location 1081075 also includes finds belonging to site 12948 (habitation of Nederweert-Rosveld 4). coordinates changed to find location 1074191, because this is the location of Nederweert-Rosveld 6 (original coordinates of find location 1081075 were inbetween the burial site and the settlement (site 12948)
five ovens for production of ceramic building material, possibly related to ROMM vicus or ROML castrum in Maastricht. no indication of habitation
vindplaats 3 from Archd 137. well and some pits dated to IJZL-ROMV. Archis find location 1099699 also contains finds related to burial site

finds indicate start date in first quarter of 2nd century, abandonment after middle of 2nd century. One (post-built) houseplan and two secondary structures situated within a ditch-enclosed area that appears similar in lay-out to villa terrains. No indications of presence of villa found
total of 270 cremation burials excavated in three campaigns, dated from roughly 250 BC to AD 250

one post-built houseplan with rooftiles (dated ROMM) and other features dating in IJZL, ROMV and ROMM. Ditch of unknown origin dated IJZL-ROMV, belonging to potential temporary camp? Also indications of presence in preceding IJZM
indications for stone-built rural settlement (rooftiles) dated in ROML, maybe even just ROMLB
one ditch with rooftiles dated to 3rd century (ADC 2700), as well as series of postholes that date to ROM (BAAC 12.0020)
large secondary structure or main building of villa, dated late 1st century-2nd century
dated 2nd century until beginning of 3rd century

features belonging to villa terrain dated from AD 170 to AD 240-250, although main building not found in trenches yet. Also sacrificial pit with same dating
aka Weert-Molenakker-Laarderweg. Habitation dated 50 BC-AD 250 (according to Archis notes, needs to be verified with reports), with 2nd century enclosure
118 cremation graves, dated from 2nd or 1st century BC to end of 2nd or beginning of 3th century AD

habitation started around beginning of 1st century and lasted into 3rd century, assumed to be 2-3 contemporary houseplans throughout this period (ZAR 39). Burial site to southwest dated to end of 2nd and beginning of 3rd century (ZAR 11)
dated ROMM based on pottery in RAAP 4326
villa dated 2nd-beginning of 3rd century, with preceding post-built settlement, dated with pottery that mostly dated around middle of 1st century
one post-built houseplan dated ROMVA-ROMMA (BAAC 13.0139) but also indications for nearby stone-built settlement (or villa) dated 2nd-3rd century (ADC 5456)

one villacomplex with three stone buildings, measuring 150x250 m. dating from last quarter of 1st century until beginning of 3rd century. Nearby ash box (also called sarcophagus), burial site of the Lady of Simpelveld, dates after AD 200

initially thought to be villa site based on rooftiles and pottery (MG 38 25) but survey showed lack of other building material, concluding that (stone-built) rural settlement is more likely (RAAP 102)
indications for pottery oven near potential villa complex

six cremation graves, at least one dated to 2nd half of 2nd century, as well as a nearby villa found later (report from excavation has gone missing). Excavation in 1942 led researcher (Bursch) to suggest presence of temple, although this was not substantiated. Report of excavation has gone missing
cremation burial with burial monument, dated 2nd century
dated to 3rd quarter of 1st century until end of 2nd century AD, total of (estimated) 75 cremation graves
mentioned in report on the ash box of Simpelveld (site 12107)

four cremation burials in ash boxes dated around AD 300

terp attested. Habitation dated from IJZM (600 BC) to 3rd century, but indications of some form of Roman military presence (or Roman military visits) especially in the (early) 1st century. possibly even very early in 12 BC, for example on the basis of a terra sigillata form that is not even found in Velsen and only found in Nijmegen and Vechten, that may have been brought in 12 BC Drusus campaign to Winsum and Bentumasiel
no finds registered in Archis, but site mentioned in report. House plan dated to 1st century, but there are indications for continued habitation nearby into 3rd century
not enough finds registered in Archis to qualify as site, but interpretation as burial site seems confident. 6 urns with cremation remains
one inhumation radiocarbon dated to AD 71-253. no other Roman features or finds described that indicate Roman habitation, so site has only been listed as burial for now

habitation existed by the start of the 1st century, unsure how much older, and ended by AD 250. not all habitation finds are associated with this site, because finds from the excavation are listed under the single find location 1081075 in Archis (attached to site 11913, the burials of Nederweert-Rosveld 5-6). also three horrea according to ZAR 53 292

coordinates set to location of southern site of Lieshout-Beekseweg. no Archis-finds associated (the Archis-find location associated with site 11285 does not distinguish the three sites, but they are distant enough to be considered separate settlements). two houseplans, one dated to 3rd century, other could not be dated more precisely than IJZL-ROM

coordinates set to location of western site of Lieshout-Beekseweg. no Archis-finds associated (the Archis-find location associated with site 11285 does not distinguish the three sites, but they are distant enough to be considered separate settlements). one houseplan dated 1st or 2nd century, 3 houseplans dated 3rd century. also probable Roman road
12 sherds and rooftile according to ZAR 53 63

ten houseplans in total (excluding one isolated one in Huifakker Zuid, which is assigned to site 11473). two houseplans dating to ROMV with one maybe even at the end of IJZL (probably not contemporary), eight others dating to ROMM(-ROML), most likely AD 200-270, probably around 3 contemporary (but not stated in report). one large square burial ditch structure that cannot be dated more precisely than ROM, but in this database set to be at least contemporary to habitation. finds not registered in Archis

temple of Nehalennia, used approximately between AD 150-250?. Possible remains of a small town with a harbour are also nearby (possibly 125 m to the east? Or to the north) supposedly belonging to a place named Ganuenta, here set to be contemporary to the supposed temple date
two houseplans dated ROMV, with one houseplan C14-dated to around middle of 1st century

excavations at Ellewoutsdijk-Steendammeweg revealed nine houseplans from middle of 1st century to middle of 2nd century, two to four contemporary homesteads. Habitation area stretches out over approximately 500 m, in two clusters 350 m apart. Site is therefore split in two (10899 and 12956), with two houseplans belonging to site 12956. these two contemporary houseplans date from roughly AD 100. coordinates moved to centre of this part of the site (vindplaats 5 in ADC 200)
temple dedicated to Nehalennia, with supposed harbour town (although tangible evidence for presence of a town is limited)

vindplaats 6 from Archd 137. ten inhumation burials mostly dated AD 400-570 (one burial has an extended dating stretching into 3rd century but is less precise). Finds are included in find location 1099699 belonging to rural settlement of site 11925 (finds from rural settlement and burial site not separated by original researchers). vindplaats 9 is a road ditch dated to 4th-5th century, the road leading up to the villa of site 11909

vindplaats 48 in Archd 137. ploughed features that likely belong to burial site dating to middle of 1st century. Coordinates of find location 1072060 not located at burial site but placed centrally in research area
